content.problemStatement:
Each palindrome can be always created from the other palindromes, since a single character is also a palindrome. For example, the string "bobseesanna" can be created by some ways:

* bobseesanna = bob + sees + anna

* bobseesanna = bob + s + ee + s + anna

* bobseesanna = b + o + b + sees + a + n + n + a

...

We want to take the value of function CountPal(s) which is the number of different ways to use the palindromes to create the string s by the above method.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T (1 ≤ T ≤ 10), the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains a non‑empty string s consisting of only lowercase English letters (1 ≤ |s| ≤ 1000).
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing CountPal(s) modulo 1 000 000 007 – the number of ways to split s into a sequence of palindromic substrings.

sampleTestCases.explanation: For "bobseesanna" there are 18 palindrome partitions, for "abba" there are 4 (a|b|b|a, a|bb|a, ab|ba, abba) and for "abc" only the trivial single‑character split.
"a" has exactly one way. "aaaaaaaaaa" (10 a's) every cut is optional, giving 2^(10‑1)=512 ways.
"abcde" contains no palindrome longer than 1, so only one partition. "aba" can be split as a|b|a or aba, thus 2 ways.

solution.approach:
For each string we pre‑compute a table isPal[i][j] (0‑based) that tells whether the substring s[i..j] is a palindrome. This can be done in O(n²) by expanding lengths: a substring of length 1 is always a palindrome, length 2 is a palindrome if the two characters are equal, and for longer substrings s[i]==s[j] && isPal[i+1][j-1].

After the table is ready we use a DP array dp where dp[i] is the number of ways to partition the prefix s[0..i‑1]. dp[0]=1 (empty prefix). For each position i (1…n) we iterate over all possible previous cut positions j (0…i‑1); if isPal[j][i‑1] is true we add dp[j] to dp[i]. All operations are performed modulo MOD = 1 000 000 007.

The answer for the test case is dp[n]. The algorithm runs in O(n²) time and O(n²) memory per test case, which easily satisfies the limits (n ≤ 1000).
solution.timeComplexity: O(T·n²) where n is the length of each string (≤1000).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(n²) for the palindrome table plus O(n) for the DP array.
